agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed change from 7-year to 90-day retention of delivery records for a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets, serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on this data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as infrastructure optimization, but it directly impacts legal compliance, contractual obligations, and core business operations.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Record Lifecycle / Data Governance",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery: consignment details, proof of delivery (POD), timestamps, signatures, chain of custody, and associated financial documents (invoices, customs declarations)." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Legal evidence that goods were delivered to the correct recipient — includes signature, photo, timestamp, GPS coordinates. Required for dispute resolution and invoice reconciliation." },
      { "term": "Consignment Note", "definition": "The contract of carriage between sender, carrier, and recipient. In cross-border EU shipments, governed by CMR Convention requiring multi-year retention." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The business and legal rules governing how long specific categories of data must be kept, balancing storage costs against legal obligations, contractual duties, and operational needs." },
      { "term": "Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The process by which B2B customers use delivery data from the platform to satisfy their own regulatory obligations (tax, customs, audit, consumer protection)." },
      { "term": "Right to Erasure", "definition": "GDPR Article 17 right, which does NOT override legal retention obligations. Data required for legal compliance or contract performance is exempt from erasure requests." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records involving cross-border EU/UK shipments must retain customs declarations and CMR consignment notes for the period required by the CMR Convention and applicable customs law (minimum 1 year for claims, but typically 3+ years for customs audit).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal obligation under international treaty (CMR Convention) and EU/UK customs law. Violation exposes the company to regulatory penalties and inability to defend against claims.",
      "testable_assertion": "No cross-border delivery record with customs documentation is deleted before the legally mandated retention period has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery records that serve as source documents for VAT transactions must be retained for the period required by the applicable tax authority — typically 6 years (UK HMRC) or 5-10 years (EU member states).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit and demand documentary evidence for the full statutory period. Destroying these records makes the company unable to substantiate VAT claims and exposes it to penalties, fines, and reassessment.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record linked to a VAT-relevant transaction is deleted before the applicable tax retention period expires."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Proof of Delivery records must be retained for at least the applicable consumer dispute and chargeback window, which in practice extends to 6 years (UK Limitation Act) or equivalent EU statute of limitations for contract disputes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without POD, the platform and its customers cannot defend against claims of non-delivery. A consumer or business customer can bring a breach-of-contract claim within the limitation period. Destroying the evidence makes every such claim indefensible.",
      "testable_assertion": "No Proof of Delivery record is deleted while a legal claim based on that delivery could still be brought under the applicable statute of limitations."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "B2B customers who rely on platform delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have access to that data for at least the duration specified in the service agreement or required by their regulatory obligations, whichever is longer.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual and commercial obligation, not a physical constraint. However, violating it would constitute breach of contract and likely trigger SLA penalties and customer churn.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record accessible to a B2B customer for compliance purposes is deleted before the contractual or regulatory retention period applicable to that customer has elapsed."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "GDPR requires that personal data not be retained longer than necessary for its purpose — but 'necessary' explicitly includes retention required by law, contract performance, or legitimate legal defense (Article 17(3)).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a legal obligation. Both over-retention and under-retention can violate GDPR. A blanket 90-day deletion without classifying data by legal basis is itself a GDPR compliance failure.",
      "testable_assertion": "Each delivery record's retention period is determined by the most restrictive applicable legal basis, not by a single blanket policy."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Active insurance claim filed 18 months after delivery", "expected_behavior": "Delivery record, POD, and chain-of-custody data must still be available to support or defend the claim. Under a 90-day policy, this evidence would have been destroyed." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC VAT audit requesting source documents for deliveries from 4 years ago", "expected_behavior": "All VAT-relevant delivery records must be producible. Failure results in tax penalties and potential reassessment of the entire period." },
    { "scenario": "B2B customer undergoes their own regulatory audit and requests delivery data from 2 years ago for compliance reporting", "expected_behavior": "Data must be available per contractual SLA. Inability to provide it constitutes breach of the service agreement and may cause the customer to fail their own audit." },
    { "scenario": "Consumer disputes non-delivery 5 months after purchase and initiates chargeback", "expected_behavior": "POD is the only defense against the chargeback. At 90 days, the evidence is already destroyed and the chargeback is uncontestable — direct financial loss." },
    { "scenario": "Cross-border delivery between UK and EU post-Brexit requires customs documentation for audit 2 years later", "expected_behavior": "Customs declarations and supporting delivery records must be retained. Destruction triggers customs compliance violations in both jurisdictions." },
    { "scenario": "GDPR subject access request or erasure request for a delivery with active legal retention obligations", "expected_behavior": "The platform must be able to distinguish which data elements can be erased vs. which must be retained under legal obligation exemptions. A blanket 90-day deletion makes this nuanced compliance impossible." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Direct exposure includes: (1) Inability to defend chargebacks — every disputed delivery older than 90 days becomes an automatic loss. (2) Tax authority penalties for missing VAT documentation — HMRC penalties alone can reach 100% of the tax due. (3) B2B contract breach penalties and SLA violations. (4) Potential GDPR fines for systematic non-compliance with retention obligations (up to 4% of global annual turnover). Storage cost savings are trivial compared to even a single regulatory enforcement action.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. The platform loses the ability to perform root cause analysis on delivery failures beyond 90 days, degrading service quality improvement. Dispute resolution teams lose access to evidence. B2B customers lose access to compliance data, generating support volume and contract renegotiations. The legal team loses the ability to defend litigation.",
    "user": "HIGH NEGATIVE. B2C consumers lose proof of delivery for warranty and dispute purposes. B2B customers — who chose this platform partly for data availability — lose a critical compliance capability, likely triggering migration to competitors who maintain proper retention.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. This change creates simultaneous violations across multiple regulatory frameworks (GDPR, VAT law, customs law, CMR Convention) in multiple jurisdictions (UK + 27 EU member states). It converts a compliant operation into a systematically non-compliant one. The risk is not theoretical — tax audits, customs audits, and consumer disputes are routine events in logistics, not edge cases.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "B2B logistics customers", "impact": "Lose access to delivery data needed for their own compliance reporting. May fail their own audits. Likely to churn or pursue breach-of-contract claims." },
      { "group": "B2C consumers", "impact": "Lose proof of delivery for disputes, warranty claims, and chargebacks after 90 days." },
      { "group": "Finance / Tax team", "impact": "Unable to substantiate VAT positions during audits. Exposed to penalties and reassessments." },
      { "group": "Legal / Compliance team", "impact": "Unable to defend claims or produce records for regulatory requests. Systematic compliance violations across multiple frameworks." },
      { "group": "Dispute resolution / Customer support", "impact": "Lose all evidence for disputes older than 90 days. Chargeback win rate drops to near zero for older deliveries." },
      { "group": "Operations / Quality team", "impact": "Lose ability to analyze historical delivery performance, identify patterns, and improve service quality." },
      { "group": "The company itself (corporate liability)", "impact": "Board-level exposure. Knowingly destroying legally required records can constitute an offence in several EU/UK jurisdictions." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 42,
    "reasoning": "Confidence is low because the proposal as stated is fundamentally contradictory — it claims 'no business logic changes' while proposing to destroy data that underpins legal compliance, contractual obligations, dispute resolution, and customer-facing features. Without knowing the specific EU member states, exact B2B contract terms, and the company's VAT registration status in each jurisdiction, I cannot define precise retention periods. However, I am highly confident that 90 days is grossly insufficient under ANY reasonable interpretation of the applicable legal frameworks. The proposal cannot be accepted as described.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC requires 6-year retention for VAT-relevant records — this is black-letter law",
      "Consumer dispute and chargeback windows universally exceed 90 days",
      "B2B customers relying on this data for compliance reporting will be materially harmed",
      "GDPR does not mandate deletion of data required for legal obligations — in fact it requires retention",
      "CMR Convention governs cross-border carriage records with multi-year claim windows"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods vary by EU member state — specific jurisdictions not identified",
      "Specific B2B contractual terms and SLA commitments unknown",
      "Whether delivery records are the sole source for VAT documentation or duplicated elsewhere",
      "Current data architecture — whether a tiered approach (hot/warm/cold storage) could achieve cost savings without deletion"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Delivery records are the primary or sole source of proof-of-delivery and VAT documentation",
      "B2B customer contracts include data availability commitments",
      "The platform handles cross-border EU/UK deliveries subject to customs requirements",
      "No separate archival system preserves legally required data outside the proposed deletion scope"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but auto-deleting delivery records after 90 days directly violates multiple legal retention requirements (VAT: 5-10 years, UK Limitation Act: 6 years, customs: 3+ years, CMR Convention: 1-3 years). This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the company's legal compliance posture. The framing itself is dangerously misleading.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Tax", "B2B customers", "B2C consumers", "Board/Corporate governance"],
      "options": [
        "A) Reject the 90-day blanket deletion entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B) Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while maintaining legally required retention periods — e.g., 90 days hot, remainder in cold/archival storage",
        "C) Implement data classification: separate PII from legally required business records, apply GDPR minimization to PII while retaining business records for the legally mandated period",
        "D) Engage legal counsel to define per-jurisdiction, per-data-category retention schedules, then implement differential retention policies"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option D (with B as an immediate cost-saving measure). The current 7-year policy may itself be imprecise — a proper legal review should define exact retention by data category and jurisdiction. Meanwhile, tiered storage (Option B) can deliver the cost savings the engineering team wants WITHOUT destroying legally required records. Option A is the safe default if legal review is delayed."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Need to know: (1) Which specific EU member states the platform operates in, as retention periods vary. (2) Exact B2B contract terms regarding data availability. (3) Whether delivery records are the sole source of truth for VAT/customs documentation or if duplicates exist. (4) Current storage architecture and cost breakdown — the real cost driver may be addressable without deletion.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Legal", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": ["Gather jurisdiction list and contract terms before any policy change", "Engage DPO and legal counsel for a formal retention impact assessment"],
      "recommendation": "No retention policy change should proceed without a formal legal impact assessment covering all operating jurisdictions."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys VAT source documents required for 5-10 years by EU/UK tax law", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys Proof of Delivery needed to defend disputes and chargebacks for up to 6 years", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days removes data that B2B customers depend on for their own regulatory compliance reporting", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "A blanket deletion policy without data classification violates GDPR's requirement for purpose-specific retention analysis", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "Auto-deletion at 90 days destroys customs and CMR Convention documents required for cross-border shipment audits", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" },
    { "violation": "The proposal is mislabeled as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters the company's compliance posture across multiple legal frameworks", "business_rule_broken": "All" }
  ]
}
